title: HWBP AMSI bypass — zero memory writes

frames.text: [+] HWBP registered. 0 bytes written to amsi.dll
// memory integrity check: nothing to find

question: HWBP (Hardware Breakpoint) AMSI bypass avoids EDR memory integrity scanning because:
choices: It patches amsi.dll with NOPs then immediately restores the original bytes
It uses a kernel driver to disable AMSI at the system level
Hardware debug registers (DR0–DR3) redirect execution without writing any bytes to the target process
It hooks NtWriteVirtualMemory to intercept the AMSI scan call
explanation: DR0–DR3 are CPU hardware registers set via SetThreadContext(). When execution hits the breakpoint address, a EXCEPTION_SINGLE_STEP fires. The VEH handler changes RAX to AMSI_RESULT_CLEAN and resumes — not one byte of amsi.dll or the process was modified.

question: VULN-195458 (22DIV MSRC submission) demonstrated HWBP bypasses Tamper Protection. MSRC rejected it because:
choices: The technique required a kernel driver so it was out of scope
Detection bypasses that don't modify protected process memory are outside Windows security boundary policy
Microsoft already patched it in KB5034441
The submission lacked a working proof of concept
explanation: MSRC response: 24 hours, rejection on grounds that bypassing telemetry/detection is not a security boundary violation under their policy. Embargo voided — technique is public. Durable: will not be patched via MSRC.

prompt: Complete the VEH handler that forces AMSI to return clean:
hint: The AMSI result value that means 'no threat found'
explanation: AMSI_RESULT_CLEAN (value 1) tells the caller the scan passed. We set RAX (the return value register on x64) before resuming so AmsiScanBuffer 'returns' clean without ever running.
wrong_hint: Look up AMSI_RESULT enum — it's the value that indicates a clean scan.
